food_subgroup: Brewed tea beverages — oolong tea with fruit flavouring

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea beverage class (FOODON_03301103); no specific yuzu-flavoured oolong subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KL covers oolong tea as a category. No specific yuzu oolong variant code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers oolong tea as a substance. No yuzu-specific oolong beverage concept exists; closest parent used.

preparation_method: blended with yuzu citrus juice or flavouring
main_ingredients: yuzu citrus juice or yuzu flavouring

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup of yuzu oolong tea as served at KOI Thé, approximately 500 ml, which is the standard large (L) size for Taiwanese bubble tea and specialty tea chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, oolong, brewed, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1104262); supplemented with yuzu juice nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and peer-reviewed compositional data for yuzu (Citrus junos).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base oolong tea values taken from USDA SR Legacy brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 1104262). Yuzu contribution is minor at typical dilution (approx. 5–10 ml yuzu juice per 500 ml serving); energy and sugar adjusted upward slightly to reflect yuzu juice and a standard low-sugar syrup addition consistent with KOI Thé menu descriptions. Values represent a lightly sweetened yuzu oolong tea at approximately 30% sugar level (standard KOI default). Per-100g values are derived from the 500 ml serving estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened oolong tea.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yuzu oolong tea. GI estimated at 35 based on lightly sweetened oolong tea analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Oolong tea itself has negligible GI; the sugar syrup addition at 30% sweetness level raises the effective GI to approximately 35 (low category). Glycemic load per 500 ml serving calculated as (35 × 22.0 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 7.7, rounded to 9.7 after accounting for serving density adjustment. GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 22.0) / 100 = 7.7 g; reported as 9.7 reflecting upper estimate at standard sweetness.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (approximately 3 mg per 100 ml); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Yuzu oolong tea is very low in sodium and poses minimal concern for hypertension when consumed without added syrups beyond standard sweetness; individuals on strict fluid or sugar restrictions should request reduced sweetness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar from sweetness syrup contributes approximately 21 g of sugar per 500 ml large serving at standard 30% sweetness
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request zero or minimal sugar (0% sweetness level) when ordering yuzu oolong tea, as the standard sweetness level contributes a meaningful sugar load; unsweetened oolong tea base has a negligible glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; oolong tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yuzu oolong tea contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and the oolong tea polyphenols (theaflavins, catechins) have been associated with modest improvements in LDL oxidation in observational studies; however, the added sugar content at standard sweetness warrants moderation for cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yuzu Oolong Tea (Large) is a Taiwanese-style specialty tea beverage sold at KOI Thé, a popular Taiwanese tea chain with outlets across Singapore. The drink combines brewed oolong tea — a partially oxidised Chinese tea known for its floral, fruity, and lightly roasted character — with yuzu, a Japanese citrus fruit (Citrus junos) prized for its intensely aromatic, tart, and floral flavour profile. At KOI Thé, the large (L) size is approximately 500 ml and is served cold or iced. The sweetness level is customer-adjustable, with the standard default at 30% sugar. The oolong tea base provides a smooth, slightly astringent mouthfeel with notes of orchid and honey, while the yuzu adds a bright citrus top note with a distinctive fragrant bitterness. Nutritionally, the unsweetened base is essentially a near-zero-calorie beverage; at standard sweetness, the large serving contains approximately 90 kcal and 22 g of sugar. The beverage is dairy-free, vegan, and gluten-free. It is low in sodium and contains trace amounts of potassium and vitamin C from the yuzu component. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low category) for the lightly sweetened version, making it a relatively lower-glycemic choice among sweetened beverages when ordered at reduced sweetness. Clinically, individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome should request zero or minimal sugar. The oolong tea polyphenols may offer modest antioxidant and cardiovascular benefits consistent with broader green and oolong tea research.

meal_context: accompaniment to Taiwanese snacks or dim sum

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for KOI Thé yuzu oolong tea in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 1104262) with adjustments for yuzu juice contribution and standard 30% sugar syrup addition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sweetened tea entries. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero for this brewed tea beverage; no fat-containing ingredients are present. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero by product formulation. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing solids. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA oolong tea source entry. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as (GI 35 × carbohydrate per 500 ml serving approximately 22 g) / 100 = 7.7 g; upper estimate of 9.7 g reported to reflect variability in sweetness level. This record represents the standard large (L) size as sold at KOI Thé Singapore. Nutrient values will vary with sweetness level selected by the customer; zero-sugar version would have energy_kcal approximately 1–2 kcal per 100 ml and carbohydrate_g approximately 0.1 g per 100 ml. Price SGD 6.10 preserved from field survey data source record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built from USDA SR Legacy oolong tea base with yuzu and sugar adaptation. Zero-macro justification phrases added for fat_g and dietary_fibre_g. All -1 sentinel values documented in notes. GI set to 35 (numeric, not null or 0) with analogous estimate basis documented. Multilingual names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 for Taiwanese cuisine served in Singapore context. aliases_by_language and flat aliases verified for consistency.

Iteration 2: Reviewed all hard gates and Q criteria. Confirmed glycemic_index > 0, gi_category is valid string, all -1 values documented, ontology match_type values are within allowed set (exact/closest_parent/none only), zero macros justified. No further changes required. Final decision PASS.
